metadata.positioning: triple-track learning app for shell, C, Python and AI literacy

tracks.title: Shell 0 to Hero
tracks.summary: Linux-first recovery track before Piscine pressure.
tracks.why_it_matters: Rebuild confidence, command fluency, filesystem intuition and process awareness.

modules.title: Navigation and files
modules.deliverable: Navigate, create, copy and clean files without hesitation.
objectives: Move confidently in a filesystem hierarchy
Create, copy, move and remove files and directories
Read and interpret absolute vs relative paths
Understand the role of hidden files and dotfiles
exit_criteria: Complete a blind navigation exercise without errors
Recreate a given directory tree from a textual spec
Explain the difference between cp and mv in own words

resources.label: 42 Lausanne - pedagogie
resources.why: Official description of the 42 learning model and autonomy expectations

resources.label: Linux command line basics - Ubuntu
resources.why: Step-by-step introduction to filesystem navigation on Linux

modules.title: Redirections and pipes
modules.deliverable: Compose useful shell chains and inspect text quickly.
objectives: Distinguish stdin, stdout and stderr
Redirect output to files using > and >>
Chain commands with pipes to filter and transform data
Use grep to search text patterns in files and streams
exit_criteria: Build a 3-stage pipeline that filters, transforms and counts lines
Redirect stderr to a log file while keeping stdout visible
Explain what happens when you pipe into grep vs redirect into grep

resources.label: 42 Lausanne - pedagogie
resources.why: Piscine shell exercises rely heavily on redirections and pipes

resources.label: GNU Bash manual - Redirections
resources.why: Authoritative reference for redirect syntax and behavior

modules.title: Permissions and execution
modules.deliverable: Understand why a script runs or fails.
objectives: Read and interpret rwx permission triads
Use chmod in symbolic and octal notation
Understand user/group/other ownership model
Explain how PATH determines which binary runs
exit_criteria: Diagnose a permission denied error and fix it with chmod
Make a script executable and run it via PATH
Explain why a file with 644 cannot be executed

resources.label: 42 Lausanne - pedagogie
resources.why: Permissions are tested early in the Piscine shell exercises

resources.label: File permissions and attributes - ArchWiki
resources.why: Clear reference for Unix permission model with practical examples

modules.title: Linux work habits
modules.deliverable: Operate daily in Linux without depending on GUI reflexes.
objectives: Use find to locate files by name, type and modification time
Navigate man pages to answer questions autonomously
Perform basic editing in vim (insert, save, quit, search)
Use git init, add, commit and log for personal versioning
exit_criteria: Find all .c files modified in the last 24 hours in a project tree
Answer a shell question using only man without web search
Create a git repository and make 3 meaningful commits

resources.label: 42 Lausanne - pedagogie
resources.why: 42 expects students to use man and vim from day one

resources.label: Git - the simple guide
resources.why: Minimal practical introduction to git basics

resources.label: Open Vim - interactive tutorial
resources.why: Hands-on vim practice without setup

tracks.title: C / Core 42
tracks.summary: Low-level rigor track aligned with the core 42 mindset.
tracks.why_it_matters: Build algorithmic reasoning, memory discipline and debugging habits.

modules.title: Syntax and control flow
modules.deliverable: Write small programs cleanly and compile with warnings enabled.
objectives: Declare and use variables with appropriate types
Write conditional logic with if/else and switch
Use while, for and do-while loops correctly
Organize code into functions with proper prototypes
Understand header files and include guards
exit_criteria: Write a program that compiles with -Wall -Wextra -Werror and zero warnings
Implement a function that uses loops and conditionals to solve a stated problem
Split a program across multiple files with a shared header

resources.label: 42 Lausanne - pedagogie
resources.why: C is the backbone of the 42 core curriculum

resources.label: norminette
resources.why: Official 42 coding style checker — all C code must pass norminette

resources.label: C programming - Beej's Guide
resources.why: Comprehensive free C guide with practical examples

modules.title: Pointers and memory
modules.deliverable: Explain memory decisions and avoid basic leaks.
objectives: Understand the difference between stack and heap allocation
Use pointers to pass data by reference
Manipulate arrays and strings through pointer arithmetic
Allocate and free dynamic memory without leaks
Recognize and avoid common pitfalls: dangling pointers, double free, buffer overflow
exit_criteria: Draw a memory diagram for a program with both stack and heap variables
Write a function that allocates, uses and frees memory correctly
Run valgrind on own code and fix all reported leaks

resources.label: 42 Lausanne - pedagogie
resources.why: Memory management is central to every 42 C project

resources.label: norminette
resources.why: Style compliance required for all 42 C submissions

resources.label: Valgrind quick start
resources.why: Essential tool for detecting memory leaks in C programs

modules.title: Build, tests and debug
objectives: Compile with strict warning flags and interpret compiler messages
Write and run a basic Makefile
Use gdb to set breakpoints, inspect variables and step through code
Use valgrind to detect memory errors and leaks
Adopt a systematic debugging workflow instead of random edits
exit_criteria: Fix a segfault using gdb without modifying code randomly
Write a Makefile with all, clean, fclean and re targets
Produce a valgrind-clean run on a project with dynamic allocation

resources.label: 42 Lausanne - pedagogie
resources.why: All 42 projects require Makefile and clean compilation

resources.label: norminette
resources.why: Code style validation required before any 42 submission

resources.label: GDB tutorial - University of Maryland
resources.why: Practical introduction to gdb for C debugging

resources.label: Valgrind quick start
resources.why: Memory error detection reference

modules.title: Library and algorithm bridge
modules.deliverable: Prepare for libft, printf, gnl and push_swap logic.
objectives: Understand the purpose of building a personal C library
Apply consistent naming and API design for reusable functions
Implement basic sorting algorithms and reason about their complexity
Develop the discipline of testing each function in isolation
exit_criteria: Implement 5 libft-style utility functions with consistent prototypes
Explain the time complexity of at least two sorting algorithms
Write a test harness that validates function behavior against edge cases

resources.label: 42 Lausanne - pedagogie
resources.why: libft is the first graded project in the 42 common core

resources.label: norminette
resources.why: All libft functions must pass norminette

resources.label: libftTester
resources.why: Community tester widely used to validate libft implementations

resources.label: awesome-42
resources.why: Curated index of 42 project guides, testers and community resources

tracks.title: Python + AI
tracks.summary: Python foundations plus the modern AI axis now present in 42 Lausanne.
tracks.why_it_matters: Prepare for the new branch without weakening shell and C fundamentals.

modules.title: Python foundations
modules.deliverable: Write and explain small scripts confidently.
objectives: Use Python types, variables and basic data structures
Write functions with clear inputs and outputs
Iterate with for and while loops over collections
Understand list comprehensions and dictionary usage
Run and test scripts from the command line
exit_criteria: Write a script that reads a file, processes its content and outputs a result
Explain the difference between a list, tuple and dictionary
Solve a small algorithmic problem using only standard library

resources.label: 42 Lausanne - IA
resources.why: Official page describing the AI and Python axis in the new common core

resources.label: Python official tutorial
resources.why: The canonical Python tutorial from the language maintainers

modules.title: Objects and automation
modules.deliverable: Build useful utilities and simple models.
objectives: Define classes with attributes, methods and constructors
Read and write files in text and JSON formats
Parse command-line arguments with argparse
Structure a small project with modules and imports
Apply basic error handling with try/except
exit_criteria: Build a CLI tool that reads JSON input, processes it and writes structured output
Design a class hierarchy for a simple domain model
Write a script that handles missing files and bad input gracefully

resources.label: 42 Lausanne - IA
resources.why: Python OOP skills feed directly into the AI projects in the new curriculum

resources.label: Python official tutorial - Classes
resources.why: Official reference for Python class syntax and OOP patterns

modules.title: AI, RAG and agent literacy
modules.deliverable: Use AI as a disciplined tool without outsourcing understanding.
objectives: Understand the retrieval-augmented generation pattern
Design prompts that produce reliable and verifiable outputs
Evaluate AI responses against source documents
Distinguish between AI assistance and AI dependency
Apply source governance: official facts vs community interpretation
exit_criteria: Build a minimal RAG pipeline that retrieves context and generates an answer
Evaluate 5 AI-generated responses for factual accuracy against sources
Explain the source-governance tiers and why they matter for learning integrity

resources.label: 42 Lausanne - IA
resources.why: Official positioning of AI literacy within the 42 Lausanne curriculum

resources.label: 42 Lausanne - pedagogie
resources.why: AI usage must remain compatible with the 42 autonomy-first learning model

resources.label: awesome-42
resources.why: Community reference for AI-related 42 projects and resources

legacy_common_core.summary: Historically C and Unix heavy, then concurrency, graphics, C++, network and devops.

new_common_core_interpretation.summary: Algorithms earlier, Python earlier, AI inside the common core, still grounded in systems thinking.
new_common_core_interpretation.note: Project names beyond official public category statements come from infographic interpretation and community mapping.
